category_title: First-grade struggling readers who experience cognitive overload and blank out when initiating speech-to-text dictation
student_description: David is a first-grade struggling reader who experiences severe cognitive overload when transitioning from brainstorming to using speech recognition (SR) technology. The mechanical act of launching the app causes him to 'go blank' and forget his ideas, and he quickly abandons tasks or deletes entire drafts when faced with transcription errors or cumbersome editing tools. He uniquely needs immediate verbal scaffolding to hold his ideas in working memory, swift teacher intervention to bypass technical hurdles, and a shift away from using SR for full-sentence drafting toward generating word banks and messy drafts.

criteria.rationale: David's working memory drops his ideas the moment he interacts with the SR interface, so he needs his exact words fed back to him immediately to prevent task abandonment.
criteria.check: The tutor provides an exact verbal reminder of the phrase David just said, or offers to dictate it for him, rather than just prompting him to 'keep trying'.

criteria.rationale: Forcing David to use native iPad editing tools (like the cursor and backspace) causes him to delete his entire draft in frustration.
criteria.check: When an SR error occurs, the tutor models thinking aloud about the word's sounds or spelling instead of instructing David to manually navigate the device's text editing tools.

criteria.rationale: David loses interest quickly when technology fails or produces inappropriate text, requiring the tutor to manage the interface for him.
criteria.check: The tutor swiftly intervenes to manage, delete, or bypass disruptive SR transcriptions rather than letting David struggle with the interface.
